turns.text: I -- I had supposed that's -- I had supposed that's a real complaint that under the United States Constitution, you can exhibit without a license.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: You can exhibit?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: You can?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: One can exhibit without a license.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: We say one can exhibit without a discretionary license. I don't know that if these were purely non-discretionary license which is what would be left to the ordinance if our position is sustained, I don't know that the Constitution forbids a purely administrative non-discretionary type of license (Inaudible).

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: Serving what purpose? Revenue or what?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Revenue, registration --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: I suppose police purposes.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Police purposes --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: I want to know where the pictures (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: They want to know where the picture is being exhibited.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Right. Now, we --

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: (Inaudible) do that?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Well, my second reason?

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: They were --

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: There were purely alternative counts. Our first count asked this city to -- that a mandatory injunction be issued directing the city to issue a permit. Now, that was the second reason for this procedure. This is the judicial gloss that was placed up in this ordinance by Illinois' Highest Court, the Illinois Supreme Court in the American Civil Liberties Union where these issues were squarely raised where the constitutional question was decided adversely to our position. We freely admit that. And there, the Illinois Supreme Court said that were the petitioner has a complaint to make about some section of the ordinance, he can come in by way of injunction proceedings seeking to enjoin the Mayor or mandamus, they said either one, seeking to enjoin the Mayor and the police commissioner to issue them the permit that they request. And I quote, if I may, from that part of the case whether --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What's the citation of that?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: It's the American Civil Liberties Union versus City of Chicago, 3 Illinois 2nd 334 page 352, it's quoted on page 11 of our brief." If the plaintiff however," I'm quoting now, "alleges that one or more of the conditions required for a license are invalid, he may proceed either by mandamus or by injunction. Whether a complaint alleging only that a motion picture is not obscene would state a ground for equitable relief, we need not decide." In the present case, the plaintiff does not (Inaudible) at their complaint for they asserted that the ordinance was invalid and claimed the right to exhibit their -- their film without obtaining a permit.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's just count two here, isn't it?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: That's correct and the state court there held that the ordinance was constitutional against the subject and therefore, that -- that is, I say, the highest --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But I am --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: -- interpretation placed on.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- honestly, I don't see how if you claim the ordinance is -- is unconstitutional, how that would -- how you'd only be involved in the state ground.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: We claim that the requirement of the ordinance that says you must submit your picture in advance is unconstitutional.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I -- I understand. I thought that was your claim there.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's not a state ground.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: No, but they -- they denied our federal ground and this Court denied to review that case, therefore, one of our final judgment because there were both grounds involved there. Maybe I can explain at this way, Your Honor. The state court acted exactly contrary to the way the federal court would act in the similar situation. The federal court would say if there is a non-constitutional question to be reached and decided and that disposes of the case, we will not decide the constitutional question, if I understand my legal precedence correct. And therefore, once the non-constitutional question is decided, that's the end of the case. The Illinois Supreme Court did not act that way, they decided both questions. They decided the non -- both the constitutional question and then send it back for a finding on the non-constitutional question. That's what they did in that case.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Please tell me how if you were exhibiting this film tomorrow and you were directed or you were brought in to court for (Inaudible).

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And you claim the invalidity of the scheme as an entirety.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: As an entirety, then you are correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How could you possibly not be raising a federal question?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: We might be raising a federal question but I'm afraid it would be decided adversely to us because --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But why?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, it would be decided adversely but then you'd come up here.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: But I think that the revenue section of this ordinance is constitutional.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you -- you don't -- if you're prosecuted for that, you -- you say you make no objection. The controversy isn't whether to pay $5 or $10 or $3, whatever it is. The controversy is about showing it without a permit. Not --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: But --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- without having paid $3.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: But you can't get a permit unless you pay the money.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes, but -- and you're not in court because you don't want to pay the money, you're in court because you don't want to commit the film.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: All I can say there again is that the city themselves conceded that if we showed this movie without a permit, we would be prosecuted on the ground that we didn't have a permit, not that we didn't submit the picture, but that we didn't have a permit.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And that's right.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: And --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And you would then answer that you haven't got a permit because you think the Constitution of the United State stage you from having a permit.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Only as to certain requirements of this ordinance, Your Honor.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: But suppose you were to file your application along with -- with $25 now, what -- what --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: -- what is (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Well, it varies. It's $3 per (Inaudible) fee.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: In cash. Where is the application on (Inaudible).

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: And then they denied (Inaudible)

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: Maybe you'll go ahead and (Inaudible) distributors and they (Inaudible) what do you think would be your defense there?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: I suppose at that point, you might be able to raise the same defense. I think the question is, is there something magical over that method of procedure over the one we did which is almost like years except that at the point where they said, "You can't have the permit because you won't show us the picture," instead of going ahead and showing the film, we then took the further review that the ordinance authorizes under the interpretation of the Illinois Supreme Court and they further review, there has been authorized by this Court in other cases. We cited the Cargill case in our brief wherein a non-First Amendment kind of a case where the person was entitled to come up and appeal after it offered to comply with the constitutional portions, refused to comply with the unconstitutional portions, it was then permitted to -- to prosecute its appeal to have the unconstitutional portion declared invalid.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The question isn't what the Supreme Court of Illinois would be, the question is whether this Court with the case that you came here on a prosecution in which you tendered the $3 over $30 and they said, "We want to see the film" and you said, "I have nothing to do with it." You then showed it in a prosecutor, the first thing that this one I'm considering, the presence is whether it would be -- whether this Court was conceivably vague, that if you satisfied your portions of the ordinance that had nothing to do with the free speech trade that you couldn't come up here to raise the free speech trade. It's just not conceivable.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Can I answer this way, Your Honor, that I'm not sure that it would be any clearer case than this. And again, I wanted to cite this -- this Cargill case. Well, that it exactly what the petitioners did. And this Court said if the commission refused to grant a license because the applicant in the one case refused to comply with statutory provisions or with rules or regulations inconsistent with the Constitution of United States, the rights of the applicant or the license could be protected and enforced by appropriate judicial proceedings. It didn't say only in criminal proceedings for proceeding without a license, it said appropriate judicial proceeding.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: But your --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What case are you reading from?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Cargill versus Minnesota, 180 United States 452, page 468. We cited at page 8 of our brief.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: Well, your real point is that you shouldn't be forced to test the constitution -- constitutionality of the statute to go through a criminal proceeding.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: But you can do it by bringing an injunction?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Right and --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's a different point.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: No, it's the same.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's a different point. I am saying that if you did go through, you could've raised the point.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: I'm saying it's one of the --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: May I rephrase it this way then, Your Honor. I'd say its one of the risks we would take in going --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: -- to the other way.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- I'm saying -- I'm suggesting you're taking no risk.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: I'm -- we'll remember that for our future case, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, that's the only --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: No risk of review but risk of the unpleasantness of (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Of the unpleasantness --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: -- in this case.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: -- of -- of --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's a different story.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: May I ask, just -- perhaps, you -- you don't -- you don't have to answer probably. Well, I'm wondering. Why didn't you file it in the state court?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: In the state court?

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: I -- actually --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: The main --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- they said you could.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: -- the main reason is delay.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You mean it's been long delayed longer there?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Yes, federal --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You had a quicker trial in the federal court.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: It's much quicker in the federal court. The -- and --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Of course, you get many suits into the federal court. We'll get the same time (Inaudible)

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: I am sure that's correct, Your Honor, but it was the main reason here for avoiding the state court. There would have been one more level of review to go through too, of course, in the state court and it wasn't the federal system because we do have the trial courts, the appellate courts and the Illinois Supreme Court which had already ruled on this question.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Well, the Illinois Supreme Court had already ruled again on this very question. Now, I'd like to address myself to what I think is the other central issue here that's been raised. Discontinue referring to the idea that this picture must be presumed obscene. And there's a syllogism there that's very important because without the major premise that the city and the court below adapts that this picture must be presumed obscene simply because we didn't submit it. There is no basis for upholding the interference. This Court has said there are some cases where interference with speech is permitted and it has detailed the kinds of speech and kinds of situation in which such interference is tolerating such as using Kingsley books as the best example. Obscene speech is not protected by the First Amendment. Well, therefore, for them to bring themselves within the exception of the Kingsley case, they must say that this film has been presumed to be obscene. There's no finding of obscenity. Nobody ever said it was obscene except all that was before the city was the title of the movie. So then unless they say the whole art is presumed to be obscene, unless and until it's brought to -- to review and it's proven innocent --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: May I ask this, sir?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Aren't you asking for a permit to show this picture whether or not it is obscene?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Your Honor, let me say something about the permit. Justice Frankfurter asked that question. The permit is a little blue strip of paper at Chicago. It has the name of the picture, the amount of the license fee and it's signed by the city collector. It is not the city sanctioning, approving or authorizing the showing of the film. It is a permit fee. And as the -- they say it, the issuance of this little blue permit, that's all the city does when it issues the permit. The Court is not being asked to sanction or authorize the showing of this film.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How can you say the city isn't authorizing?The city certainly is authorizing.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: No more --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: If you say it isn't approving of it, I can understand but when you (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: I -- I meant authorizing in terms of --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- that's exactly --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: -- approving.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- what it's doing.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: It is not approving of the contents --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: -- of the film. It might be a very bad film. The city is not saying that it's a good film.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But its saying, isn't it, that it -- it can be shown as not obscene?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: No. The city is saying that? Not at the time it issue --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But when does it say?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: If it's -- if it's submitted, yes, you --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You raised -- you raised the question in my mind, if whether you have any case at all (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: No, no. I --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- you'd say that.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: I was addressing myself to the hypothetical that Justice Whittaker asked before. Under the present ordinance --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Oh, I'm not (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: -- of course they're saying it's obscene.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) cases here I'm asking you by this very suit. You aren't asking the Court to direct the city to issue a license to show this film whether or not it's obscene.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Whether or not it's obscene because there's no reason to assume that it is obscene.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, is there a reason to assume that it is not?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: No. And therefore, it's the same thing as -- as a printing press or any other form of communication. You make no assumption about it. It's up to the city and the State to come forward with pretty convincing proof that this falls within one of the exception areas.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, suppose they -- well, are you now saying that what all this (Inaudible) about is that there's a presumption that your film satisfy -- does not offend the restriction of the ordinance and that is any such assumption is to be -- any such factor entered into the situation and the police commissioner should say, "We have very good grounds from believing that this is a dirty film and show it to us." Would you think the city was entitled to see it?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: It would be a different case, Your Honor. I don't know that'd be -- say be entitled to see it but it's not this case. Your Honor, this is the same thing that could be said --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I should think the Court (Inaudible) -- I should think the Court would say but instead of indulging into presumptions one way or another, why don't you show it? (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Well, let me get to that.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It's not one way or the other.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Your Honor, let me get to the reasons why we won't show it. And that really is -- is the other part of this defense that the court below has put up.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You'd say it would be a different case if the police commissioner said the fact of the matter is I've had applied a showing of it and based on that, I think I wanted to do the film.Do you think that would be a different case?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: It would not be this case. I know that'd be decided the same as this case or not. But I'd like to say the reasons, if I may, Your Honor, why we think that this refusal to submit is important. All that the city has come up with and all that the court below has come up with is that somehow movies are different than newspaper. No, the Chicago Tribune can't be submitted -- required to submit to this requirement but motion pictures can. Why? And all we get -- come up with -- and I kept writing down the different answers that the city gave to these questions both here and of the Court of Appeals, the only one I finally distilled out is that it's shown in the dark. Well, it seems to me that they're using a pretty big shotgun because of their fear of the dark. The fact of the matter is that a motion picture is a communication at least it has to be presumed to be one.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, you want to raise this abstract question, is that it?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: No, let me say why it's not abstract, Your Honor. Let me --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You just stated the --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: -- say the things that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- you just stated --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: What?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- as an abstract proposition, that you --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: What?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- wanted to get after this -- this question of the dark and you think that doesn't appeal to your reasoning powers but if the police commissioner had good grounds for thinking it was dirty, then you think it would be a difference case.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Your Honor, in Kingsley books, which is the furthest this Court has ever gone, the Court relied on a specific finding indeed I think an admission of pornography to justify the extraordinary interference with freedom of speech. We say there must be something comparable to that. I'd like to hope, Your Honor, that it will be at very -- the very least that requirement. But we're saying that at least there must be something comparable to that requirement met in a case before the interference can be justified. Just at what point the Court is going to draw that line is a hard question for me to answer, Your Honor. As I say, I hope it would not go beyond the point it was drawn in the Kingsley Books case. But certainly, even in that case which drew the line further than it had ever been drawn before, there was a firm and positive finding by a court that the matter was obscene.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But in the Near against Minnesota, it was conceded that the stuff was -- was vicious stuff and the doctrine was laid down but they can't have prior censorship of loose cases. Therefore, you, yourself have already indicated the difference between films and newspapers --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Well, I'd like to --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- because you've said there ought to be something.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: No, no, Your Honor. If I said that, then -- then I misspoke. I said I can't answer where this Court would draw that line. It is a different case than this case. And conceivably, the Court could draw the line. Yes, some kind of finding is alright but no, this kind of shotgun approach is not. That is the point I'm trying to make.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm suggesting that in Near, drawing lines would reject it.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm suggesting that in Near, drawing line would reject it with reference to newspapers.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, that's the question I thought you had up and I seem to have fully --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: That's not a --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) based on a misapprehension that you say.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: No, the question is -- the question is the same, Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What -- are you raising the question in Near versus Minnesota?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That here, this ordinance is invalid. You don't have to submit it, called -- it's a form of prior censorship --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- it is prohibited by the Constitution.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Right, Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Then what difference does it make whether you have presume one way or the other?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: I agree, Your Honor. I -- I believe --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I don't understand all (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: I am addressing --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- presumption on the question you raised.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: -- I'm addressing myself to the question that was raised by some of the other Justices. We don't think that the presumption should go one way or the other, that it is not relevant.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: I think that -- perhaps a confusion of reason by a reason of the first prayer of your relief, they may -- should get a permit because if this permit system is unconstitutional, a permit becomes irrelevant.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Purely -- well, supposed it's a purely a revenue permit.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: Well, that's a -- never -- never statute --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Well, I know --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: -- but this is --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: -- it's part of the same statute, Your Honor, that's our difficulty. Section 1553 of this very ordinance is purely and simply a revenue measure which can stand by itself as far as I can see. And if the Court would've strike -- grant our full petition for relief, that section could still stand and the city could continue to collect revenue from every motion picture who has exhibited. It's not confiscatory. We've never claimed it was.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But the suggestion if we've had a different case. If the police commissioner had good grounds for suspecting that this is a dirty picture is not a confusing suggestion but a differentiating suggestion. You say that was different. You're saying Kingsley there was a firm statement that it is pornographic.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. Now, I don't --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) case that that is a very different thinking to say we don't care whether it is pornographic. It can't be the center to begin with.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Well -- well we've never said -- the question here is that how can you say it's pornographic when you it -- when you have nothing on which to base --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm not saying it is, but I say there's a difference between inquiring into the question as the basis of censorship and saying it's irrelevant.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: May I ask you this.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: If we were to grant the relief you (Inaudible) and that became the law, then would it not follow that every motion picture exhibitor could show any picture however obscene without any ability of -- upon -- the parts of the municipality to stop it?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: No sir, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Why not?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Because the next statement that could come out of my mouth, Your Honor, could be an obscene statement. I hope it isn't. No one says that I should go to either the District of Columbia or to the City of Chicago and get a permit to -- before and tell them in advance what I'm going to say to this Court. If I do make an obscene statement, this Court and the Government of the District have ample protection to -- to punish me which in turn would deter others from making that statement and to keep me from making it in the future.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, then are you saying that the remedy is a criminal punishment?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: And that there is no other power on the part of the State to enjoin obscenity?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: The limited -- there is because this Court said so in Kingsley Books. There are certainly -- I would have to -- I'd have to say there's the limited injunction power of Kingsley Books but that was predicated, as I said before, on a finding of obscenity before that remedy was -- was enforced.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: But you won't let us look at the film.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: You haven't shown anybody the film.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: But nobody showed the book to the New York Board before the book was distributed in -- in Kingsley Books either. They want to have them and sold the book and then the police came in and read the book and decided it was obscene and persuaded the judge that it was obscene. And then the injunction was issued. Now, that's altogether different from this which says, "You submit everything to us and we will let the obscene -- we will let the unobscene go through and the obscene cannot go through. Now, but -- and this is the last point I'd like to make, what is the importance of this refusal to submit to -- to the motion picture distributor? First of all, there's a nature of the function of the censors. What way it is that the censors work? This Court has before it in the record of -- of the different case between the same parties, a description, a detailed description of how the censors work and I cite that record to you not because the Chicago censors are better than or worse than other censors but because it is the nature of the animal. Censors job is to censor.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Does the police commissioner delegate the (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- before it (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: He (Voice Overlap) censor for, just as the Mayor delegates it to his corporation (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Are they ordinarily police officers?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: No, Your Honor, they are civilian employees.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Are there police officers?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: No, there's a police sergeant who is in charge of the detail and censor board itself is usually six women or -- or occasionally a man, the civilian employees of the police department.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: A permanent body or ad hoc?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Well, they're supposedly civil service but they are given temporary appointment somewhere between permanent and ad hoc.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Do you know how many cities have more censors like this?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: About 15, Your Honor.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: And four States (Inaudible).

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Four.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Illinois is not one of the States. Chicago has the ordinance but Illinois itself does not have any kind of prior submission -- statute.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Yes, it was cited in the Motion Pictures Association brief amicus in their table at the back and that this -- all of the cities and also the kinds of reviewer that they have. The second thing I'd like to make is the difference between and perhaps this answers your question, Justice Whittaker. The difference between this kind of proceeding, which I might add has hearing procedure, has none of the due process safeguards that a criminal proceeding would have. There's no presumption of innocence. As I've indicated the presumption rather it's one of guilt. Prove your pictures clean or else you can't get a permit. So the contrary, its the criminal procedure where it's up to the prosecutor to prove to a trial court and perhaps even a grand jury in the first place and a judge, that this is obscene literature. It was said best I might add.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Are you done by due process now?

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: Due process in the sense of the kinds of protections that are afforded to speech rather than due process in the kinds of protection that are afforded the individual. I think it was best said by this very Court of Appeals, Your Honor, sitting with a different panel in a case called Capital Enterprises versus the City of Chicago, in which the Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit said there is a wide difference between censoring motion pictures before deciding that they can be publicly exhibited and exhibiting a picture to the public for which criminal punishment might be imposed. In the latter situation, all the familiar procedural safeguards come in to play while in the other instance, there are no procedural safeguards and communication is choked off at the threshold. Now, I'm quoting from page 37 of our brief. As I say this was a case where they reversed the censor board. Significantly, I might add, the other function, the demonstrations of the functions of the censors is indicated from our appendix where every single decision of there banning a picture since Burstyn has been reversed if the -- the Senate were to be alarmed. Now, the other point I'd like to make is the timeliness of the speech. Would the Declaration of Independence or the Gettysburg Address have been as effective if it has been made five years later? With the great debates that are going on right now, these are effective if they were made after November 8th, as they are right now. This case has been going on two and a half year, Your Honor. The subject matter of American Civil Liberties Union versus City of Chicago was the miracle, the same picture involved in the Burstyn case. It took five years to clear that picture under this ordinance. And significantly, it never did show in the City of Chicago. This is the kind of concern that this priority restraint brings about.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I suppose it's relevant but I just wonder how much American civilization has suffered through this segregation.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: That is the decision of the First Amendment says it should be left to the people.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: And this --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) if it covers you.

turns.name: ABNER J. MIKVA
turns.text: If it covers. I think that the only way you could say that it does cover, Your Honor, is to say that somehow this is not a speech but motion pictures communicate. They express ideas, yes, and they express advocacy. And the rights we fight for the day for Times Film is also and has the counterpart of the right to hear. And unless you can find that this is not speech, it seems to me it's governed by (Inaudible)
